title: Enzyme-Substrate Binding
description: The lock-and-key mechanism — watch enzymes bind substrates with geometric precision and catalyse reactions at impossible speed.

temperature.label: Temperature Factor
temperature.description: Higher = faster reaction but denaturing risk above 1.0

narration.text: Purple = enzymes. Yellow hexagons = substrate molecules. Enzymes are proteins folded into precise 3D shapes — their active site matches only specific substrate shapes.
Enzymes and substrates collide randomly in solution. When the shapes match perfectly, the substrate fits the active site — like a key in a lock.
Binding triggers catalysis. The enzyme strains the substrate's chemical bonds, lowering the activation energy needed to break them — from millions of years to milliseconds.
The enzyme releases the product (teal) and is instantly free to bind another substrate. One enzyme can catalyse thousands of reactions per second — unchanged.
Notice the enzyme is never consumed. This is what makes enzymes extraordinary — they enable reactions without being used up.
Drugs like aspirin, antibiotics, and HIV antiretrovirals work by fitting into enzyme active sites and blocking them — competitive inhibition, preventing the catalysis entirely.
